bibliographic_data.title: Probabilistic Relational Reasoning via Metrics
bibliographic_data.abstract: The Fuzz programming language by Reed and Pierce uses an elegant linear type system combined with a monad-like type to express and reason about probabilistic sensitivity properties, most notably ε-differential privacy. We show how to extend Fuzz to capture more general relational properties of probabilistic programs, with approximate, or (ε, δ)-differential privacy serving as a leading example. Our technical contributions are threefold. First, we introduce the categorical notion of comonadic lifting of a monad to model composition properties of probabilistic divergences. Then, we show how to express relational properties in terms of sensitivity properties via an adjunction we call the path construction. Finally, we instantiate our semantics to model the terminating fragment of Fuzz extended with types carrying information about other divergences between distributions.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper extends the Fuzz programming language to capture more general relational properties of probabilistic programs, specifically (ε, δ)-differential privacy.
core_content.key_methodology: Introduces comonadic lifting of a monad and path construction to model composition properties of probabilistic divergences and express relational properties in terms of sensitivity properties.
outcomes: Extended the Fuzz language to handle (ε, δ)-differential privacy
Developed a framework for modeling probabilistic relational properties using comonadic liftings and path constructions
core_content.full_text_markdown: In recent years, differential privacy has emerged as a robust notion of privacy. The paper aims to extend the Fuzz programming language by Reed and Pierce to capture more general relational properties of probabilistic programs, specifically (ε, δ)-differential privacy. The authors introduce comonadic lifting of a monad to model composition properties of probabilistic divergences and use an adjunction called the path construction to express relational properties in terms of sensitivity properties. This work provides a unified perspective on a class of probabilistic relational properties.
